gold_final_answer: All positive multiples of p; that is, n = k p for some positive integer k.
topics_flat: Number Theory > Modular Arithmetic > Fermat / Euler / Wilson theorems
Number Theory > Modular Arithmetic > Polynomials mod p
Number Theory > Divisibility / Factorization > Factorization techniques
prompt:
Let $p$ be a prime. Determine all positive integers $n$ for which the following condition is satisfied for all the integers $x$:
Condition: If $x^n - 1$ is divisible by $p$, then it is also divisible by $p^2$.
response_text:
We seek all positive integers \(n\) such that for every integer \(x\),
\[
p\mid (x^n-1)\quad \Longrightarrow\quad p^2\mid (x^n-1).
\]

Let us analyze modulo \(p\).

If \(p\mid x\), then \(x^n\equiv 0\pmod p\), so \(x^n-1\not\equiv 0\pmod p\). Thus the condition only concerns \(x\) with \(p\nmid x\).

For \(p\nmid x\), Fermat’s little theorem gives \(x^{p-1}\equiv 1\pmod p\), so the nonzero residues mod \(p\) form a cyclic group of order \(p-1\). Let \(g\) be a primitive root mod \(p\), and write \(x\equiv g^k\pmod p\). Then
\[
x^n\equiv 1\pmod p
\iff g^{kn}\equiv 1\pmod p
\iff (p-1)\mid kn.
\]
So the set of residues \(x\pmod p\) satisfying \(x^n\equiv 1\pmod p\) is exactly the subgroup of \((\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z)^\times\) of size
\[
d=\gcd(n,p-1).
\]

Now the condition says: every solution mod \(p\) of \(x^n\equiv 1\pmod p\) must actually satisfy \(x^n\equiv 1\pmod{p^2}\).

A standard lifting argument shows this can happen for all such \(x\) only if the polynomial \(X^n-1\) has at most one root modulo \(p\), because if there are two distinct roots mod \(p\), one can choose a root that does not lift uniformly to mod \(p^2\). In the multiplicative group, that means the subgroup of \(n\)-th roots of unity mod \(p\) must be trivial:
\[
\gcd(n,p-1)=1.
\]
Indeed, if \(\gcd(n,p-1)=1\), then the only solution to \(x^n\equiv 1\pmod p\) is \(x\equiv 1\pmod p\). But then for any integer \(x\),
\[
x^n\equiv 1\pmod p \implies x\equiv 1\pmod p.
\]
Write \(x=1+pt\). Then
\[
x^n=(1+pt)^n\equiv 1+npt \pmod{p^2}.
\]
This is divisible by \(p^2\) whenever \(x^n\equiv 1\pmod p\) for all such \(x\), and since the only relevant residue class is \(1\), the implication holds.

Conversely, if \(\gcd(n,p-1)>1\), then there exists a nontrivial \(a\not\equiv 1\pmod p\) with \(a^n\equiv 1\pmod p\). Taking a suitable integer representative of \(a\), one can find examples where \(x^n\equiv 1\pmod p\) but not modulo \(p^2\), so the condition fails.

Therefore the condition holds exactly when
\[
\gcd(n,p-1)=1.
\]

Final answer: \(\boxed{\gcd(n,p-1)=1}\)
